:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 6,227 people who were eligible for parole but remained incarcerated for a new crime. This group made up 33% of the prison population.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?

By law, a nonviolent offender convicted of a Class D felony with an aggregate sentence of one to five years is eligible for parole review after serving 15% or two months of their original sentence, whichever is longer. The time before the first parole review for all felonies committed after December 3, 1980, is determined based on the sentence being served. For sentences ranging from 1 year, up to but not including 2 years, the time before the first review is 4 months. For sentences ranging from 2 years up to and including 39 years, the time before the first review is 20% of the sentence. For sentences of 39+ years, including life sentences, the time before the first review is 8 years. In the case of a persistent felony offender with a class A, B, or C felony, the time before the first review is 10 years.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison for a new crime, accounting for 76% (4,687 people) of the parole-eligible prison population.


``` ::: ::: ### Gender ### Age ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Most Serious Sentenced Offense

In 2020, there were 3,440 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 55% of the parole-eligible prison population in prison for new crimes.

``` ::: :::

## Disparities
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Descriptive Statistics

``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Race, ethincity, age, and gender

``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Relative Rate Index

The relative rate index (RRI) provides a measure of disparity in outcomes for different racial and ethnic groups by comparing the rates at which individuals of different races and ethnic groups experience incarceration. The rate for each specific racial and ethnic group is computed by dividing the population of interest within that group by the total state population for that particular group.

To calculate the RRI, the rate of each outcome of interest for individuals who are Black non-Hispanic and Hispanic any race, is divided by the rate at which the outcome occurs for individuals who are White. A RRI of 1 indicates no disparity. RRIs greater than 1 indicate the outcome occurs more frequently for individuals who are Black or Hispanic, and an RRI less than 1 indicates an outcome occurs less frequently than for individuals who are White.
